subject: ap-calculus-bc
title: Function Analysis from Graph of Derivative
stimulus: The graph of $f'$, the derivative of a function $f$, is shown above for $-6 \leq x \leq 12$. The graph of $f'$ consists of line segments and a semicircle. It is known that $f(0) = 7$. The function $g$ is defined by $g(x) = \int_0^x f'(t)\,dt$.

parts.prompt: Find $g'(8)$. Give a reason for your answer.

scoring_points.description: By the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus, $g'(x) = f'(x)$ for all $x$. Therefore $g'(8) = f'(8)$, which is read directly from the graph of $f'$. The correct numerical value is obtained by reading the $y$-value of the graph at $x = 8$.
scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point: $g'(8) = f'(8)$ by FTC, with correct value read from graph

sample_responses.response: By FTC, $g'(x) = f'(x)$. So $g'(8) = f'(8)$, which is the $y$-value of the graph at $x=8$ (read from the graph).
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly differentiates $g(x) = \int_0^x f'(t)\,dt$ using FTC Part 1 to get $g'(x) = f'(x)$.
Incorrect. By FTC, $\frac{d}{dx}\int_0^x f'(t)\,dt = f'(x)$. The derivative of the integral of $f'$ is $f'$, not $f''$.

required_elements: $g'(x) = f'(x)$ by FTC, so $g'(8) = f'(8)$ with value read from graph

scoring_points.description: Correct reason explicitly stated: by the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus, $g'(x) = f'(x)$ for all $x$ in the domain.
scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point: reason — by the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus, $g'(x) = f'(x)$

sample_responses.response: By the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus, $g'(x) = f'(x)$.
sample_responses.commentary: Explicitly names the Fundamental Theorem as the reason.
Vague — does not cite the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus. A complete reason must state that $g'(x) = f'(x)$ by FTC.

required_elements: Cites Fundamental Theorem of Calculus as the reason

parts.prompt: Find all values of $x$ in the open interval $-6 < x < 12$ at which the graph of $g$ has a point of inflection. Give a reason for your answer.

scoring_points.description: Correctly identifies that points of inflection of $g$ occur where $g'' = (f')' = f''$ changes sign, equivalently where $f'$ changes from increasing to decreasing or from decreasing to increasing (i.e., where $f'$ has a local extremum or a corner that causes a sign change in its slope).
scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point: inflection points of $g$ occur where $g''=(f')'$ changes sign, i.e., where $f'$ changes monotonicity

sample_responses.response: Since $g'(x) = f'(x)$, $g''(x) = (f')'(x) = f''(x)$. Inflection points of $g$ occur where $g''$ changes sign, i.e., where $f'$ changes from increasing to decreasing or vice versa.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly identifies that $g'' = f''$ and that inflection points correspond to sign changes of $f''$, which are visible as direction changes of $f'$ on the graph.

sample_responses.response: Inflection points of $g$ occur where $g'(x) = f'(x) = 0$.
sample_responses.commentary: Confuses zeros of $g'$ (critical points of $g$) with inflection points. Inflection points require a sign change of $g''$, which occurs where $f'$ changes monotonicity, not where $f'=0$.

required_elements: Inflection points of $g$ occur where $g''$ changes sign, i.e., where $f'$ changes monotonicity

scoring_points.description: Identifies all correct $x$-values in $(-6, 12)$ where $f'$ changes from increasing to decreasing or vice versa (these are the inflection points of $g$). The student must list all such values read from the graph and provide the reason that $g'' = f''$ changes sign at each.
scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point: lists all $x$-values in $(-6,12)$ where $f'$ changes monotonicity (read from graph), with reason that $g''$ changes sign

sample_responses.response: From the graph, $f'$ changes from decreasing to increasing (or increasing to decreasing) at $x = \ldots$ (values read from graph). These are the inflection points of $g$ because $g'' = f''$ changes sign at each.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly reads all direction-change points from the graph of $f'$ and links them to sign changes of $g''$.

sample_responses.response: Only the point where the semicircle is centered is an inflection point of $g$.
sample_responses.commentary: Identifies only one direction-change of $f'$ instead of all of them. All points where $f'$ changes monotonicity (whether at corners or smooth peaks/valleys) are inflection points of $g$.

required_elements: All correct $x$-values where $f'$ changes monotonicity, with reason that $g''$ changes sign

scoring_points.description: Correct value of $g(0)$: by definition, $g(0) = \int_0^0 f'(t)\,dt = 0$.

sample_responses.commentary: The lower and upper limits are both $0$, so the integral of any integrable function over a degenerate interval is $0$.
Confuses $g(0)$ with $g'(0) = f'(0)$. $g(0) = \int_0^0 f'(t)\,dt = 0$, not the value of $f'$ at $0$.

scoring_points.description: Correct value of $g(-6)$: $g(-6) = \int_0^{-6} f'(t)\,dt = -\int_{-6}^0 f'(t)\,dt$. The student must correctly compute the signed area under $f'$ from $-6$ to $0$ using the geometric shapes visible on the graph (triangles, rectangles, semicircles), then negate it. The correct numerical value follows from accurately reading the graph.
scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point: $g(-6) = -\int_{-6}^0 f'(t)\,dt$ with correct geometric area calculation from the graph

sample_responses.response: $g(-6) = \displaystyle\int_0^{-6}f'(t)\,dt = -\displaystyle\int_{-6}^0 f'(t)\,dt$. Reading the signed area under $f'$ on $[-6,0]$ from the graph using the geometric shapes gives the correct value.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly reverses the limits (introducing a negative sign) and computes the geometric area from the graph.

sample_responses.response: $g(-6) = \displaystyle\int_{-6}^0 f'(t)\,dt$ (computed from graph without sign reversal).
sample_responses.commentary: Does not apply the sign reversal from flipping integration limits. $g(-6) = \int_0^{-6} f' = -\int_{-6}^0 f'$; omitting the negation gives the wrong sign.

required_elements: Correct numerical value of $g(-6)$ using $g(-6) = -\int_{-6}^0 f'(t)\,dt$ and geometric area calculation

parts.prompt: Find the value of $x$ at which $g$ attains an absolute minimum on the closed interval $-6 \leq x \leq 12$. Justify your answer.

scoring_points.description: Correctly identifies the candidates for the absolute minimum: critical points of $g$ (where $g'(x) = f'(x) = 0$ or $g'$ is undefined in the interior $(-6, 12)$) and the endpoints $x = -6$ and $x = 12$.
scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point: identifies candidates — zeros of $f'$ in $(-6,12)$ plus endpoints $x=-6$ and $x=12$

sample_responses.response: Critical points of $g$ occur where $g'(x) = f'(x) = 0$. Reading from the graph, $f'(x)=0$ at $x = \ldots$ (values from graph). Also check endpoints $x=-6$ and $x=12$.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly applies the Closed Interval Method: identify all interior critical points (where $f'=0$) and both endpoints.

sample_responses.response: Minimum occurs where $g'(x)=f'(x)$ changes from negative to positive.
sample_responses.commentary: This identifies only local minima in the interior, missing the endpoints. For an absolute minimum on a closed interval, all candidates (interior critical points AND endpoints) must be considered.

required_elements: Sets $g'(x) = f'(x) = 0$ to find critical points and also considers endpoints $x = -6$ and $x = 12$

scoring_points.description: Computes $g$-values at all candidate points by accumulating signed area under $f'$ from the graph (using geometric areas of triangles, rectangles, and the semicircle), correctly applying $g(x) = \int_0^x f'(t)\,dt$.
scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point: evaluates $g$ at all candidate $x$-values using $g(x) = \int_0^x f'(t)\,dt$ and geometric area computation

sample_responses.response: At each candidate $x$: $g(x) = \int_0^x f'(t)\,dt$ = (accumulated signed area from $0$ to $x$, computed from graph geometry).
sample_responses.commentary: Applies $g(x) = \int_0^x f'(t)\,dt$ at each candidate, using triangular, rectangular, and semicircular areas from the graph.

sample_responses.response: $g(x) = f(x)$, so $g$ attains its minimum where $f$ is smallest.
sample_responses.commentary: $g(x) = \int_0^x f'(t)\,dt = f(x) - f(0)$, which equals $f(x)$ only if $f(0) = 0$. More importantly, comparing $g$-values requires computing the definite integrals from the graph, not just reading $f$.

required_elements: Evaluates or compares $g$-values at all candidate $x$-values using accumulated area under $f'$

scoring_points.description: Correct answer: the $x$-value at which $g$ is smallest among all candidates, with a complete justification that compares $g$-values at all critical points and both endpoints. The answer follows directly from the geometric areas read from the graph.
scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point: correct $x$-value with comparison of $g$-values at all candidates as justification

sample_responses.response: Comparing $g$ at all candidates: $g(-6)=\ldots$, $g(x_1)=\ldots$, $g(12)=\ldots$. The smallest value is at $x=\ldots$, so $g$ attains its absolute minimum there.
sample_responses.commentary: Compares all candidate values and identifies the minimum with explicit comparison.

sample_responses.response: The absolute minimum is at the interior critical point because it is a local minimum.
sample_responses.commentary: A local minimum in the interior need not be the absolute minimum on the closed interval. All endpoint values must also be compared before declaring an absolute minimum.

required_elements: Correct $x$-value with comparison of $g$ at all candidate points as justification
